subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
title: Solar Power Technologies and Agriphotovoltaics (APVs)
focus: Mechanisms of solar power generation and the dual-use model of Agriphotovoltaics.
rationale: Covers foundational solar energy technologies and the specific, highly testable concept of Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) which bridges agriculture and renewable energy.
sectionExcerpt: Solar power utilizes photovoltaic cells to produce direct current (DC), which is converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter. Other technologies include Solar Thermal Energy for heat and Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) using mirrors and lenses. Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) or Solar Farming combines agricultural production and electricity generation on the same land, utilizing solar panels, tracking systems, and shade-tolerant crops to maximize land-use efficiency.
evidenceBullets: Photovoltaic cells produce direct current (DC) which is converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter.
Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) harnesses energy by focusing sunlight using mirrors, lenses, and tracking devices.
Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) combine agricultural production and electricity production on the same land area.
APV systems utilize shade-tolerant crops to protect against strong solar irradiation and winds.

subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
title: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
focus: Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI), hydrogen color typology, and NGHM targets.
rationale: A major national mission with specific targets, nodal agencies, certification criteria, and a highly testable color-coded hydrogen typology.
sectionExcerpt: The Centre launched the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI) under NGHM, with MNRE as the ministry and the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) as the nodal authority. 'Green' certification requires an emission intensity of 2 kg CO2eq/kg H2 or less. Green hydrogen is produced via electrolysis using renewable energy. The text details various hydrogen types including Turquoise (Pyrolysis of Methane) and Pink (Electrolysis via Nuclear). NGHM targets 5 MMT per annum by 2030 and includes the SIGHT programme and Green Hydrogen Hubs like Deendayal, V.O. Chidambaranar, and Paradip ports.
evidenceBullets: The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) is the Nodal Authority for the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI).
Green Certification is conditional on meeting an average emission intensity requirement of 2 kg CO2eq/kg H2 or less.
Turquoise Hydrogen is produced through Pyrolysis using Methane, while Pink Hydrogen uses Electrolysis powered by Nuclear Energy.
NGHM targets the production of 5 MMT per annum of Green Hydrogen by 2030.
Deendayal Port, V.O. Chidambaranar Port, and Paradip Port have been recognized as Green Hydrogen Hubs under NGHM.
Green hydrogen production requires up to 9 litres of water per kg of hydrogen and demands high electricity.

subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
title: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
focus: Objectives, components, and revised financial assistance guidelines of the National Bioenergy Programme.
rationale: Important scheme for waste-to-energy and biomass utilization, featuring specific financial assistance criteria and components relevant to renewable energy and rural development.
sectionExcerpt: MNRE updated guidelines for the National Bioenergy Programme (NBP), launched in 2022 to utilize surplus biomass for power generation. It provides Central Financial Assistance (CFA), with 20% extra for special categories like the North East Region and SC/ST beneficiaries. The NBP has three components: Waste to Energy (using pyrolysis and plasma gasification), Biomass (briquettes/pellets), and Biogas (95% methane and CO2). Revised guidelines offer easier approvals, two-stage CFA disbursal, IoT monitoring, and stubble burning support.
evidenceBullets: The National Bioenergy Programme provides 20% more Central Financial Assistance (CFA) for special categories like the North East Region, hilly states, and SC/ST beneficiaries.
The Waste to Energy Programme supports projects generating Biogas, BioCNG, Power, or Syngas through technologies like pyrolysis and plasma gasification.
Biogas is composed of 95% methane (CH4) and CO2, with traces of N2, H2, H2S, and O2.
Revised guidelines for Waste to Energy introduce a two-stage CFA release: 50% after State Pollution Control Board consent and the remainder after 80% capacity achievement.
The Biomass Programme supports stubble burning mitigation by allowing pellet producers in NCR to choose between MNRE or CPCB schemes.
